elements.sent-2411-001: Stergiou \at Faculty of Electrical Engineering, Mathematics and Computer Science at the University of Twente, Drienerlolaan 5, 7522 NB Enschede, The Netherlands \and R.Poppe \at Department of Information and Computing Sciences at Utrecht University, Princetonplein 5, 3584 CC Utrecht, The Netherlands.
elements.sent-2411-002: We have witnessed impressive advances in video action understanding.
elements.sent-2411-003: Increased dataset sizes, variability, and computation availability have enabled leaps in performance and task diversification.
elements.sent-2411-004: Current systems can provide coarse- and fine-grained descriptions of video scenes, extract segments corresponding to queries, synthesize unobserved parts of videos, and predict context.
elements.sent-2411-005: This survey comprehensively reviews advances in uni- and multi-modal action understanding across a range of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-006: We focus on prevalent challenges, overview widely adopted datasets, and survey seminal works with an emphasis on recent advances.
elements.sent-2411-007: We broadly distinguish between three temporal scopes: (1) recognition tasks of actions observed in full, (2) prediction tasks for ongoing partially observed actions, and (3) forecasting tasks for subsequent unobserved action.
elements.sent-2411-008: This division allows us to identify specific action modeling and video representation challenges.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, we outline future directions to address current shortcomings.

elements.sent-2411-010: For decades, analyzing human actions in videos has been of particular interest to the computer vision community.
elements.sent-2411-011: Videos are prominent in both our social and professional lives.
elements.sent-2411-012: Over time, the analysis of actions has shifted from the well-understood task of action recognition towards the fundamental and broader area of action understanding.
elements.sent-2411-013: Shown in \Cref{fig:roadmap , action understanding now includes diverse tasks based on prediction and anticipation with multimodal inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The unique challenges and novel computation paradigms are the core focus of our survey.
elements.sent-2411-015: In developmental psychology, action understanding has been explored across several psychological aspects \citep{thompson2019conceptualizing : The ability to understand the action performed relates to differentiating between analogous actions \citep{gallese1996action,jeannerod1994representing and conceptualizing how an action is performed \citep{spunt2011identifying .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Determining the goal of the action has been studied in the context of immediate goals \citep{calvo2005action,kohler2002hearing,rizzolatti2001neurophysiological in relation to motor functions for the execution of actions and the sensory perception of actions performed by others.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Determining the actor's intention refers to identifying high-level goals and motivations to perform actions \citep{kilner2011more .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Intentions have been defined as the sequential grouping of individual actions \citep{fogassi2005parietal and their abstract associated target \citep{uithol2011understanding .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Inspired by the cognitive aspects of action understanding, we define three broad temporal scopes to group seminal machine vision action understanding tasks.
elements.sent-2411-020: We visualize action sequence progression in~\Cref{fig:tasks with a currently (partially) performed action followed by a subsequent action.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Tasks that require an action to be observed in full are broadly referred to as recognition tasks and infer information such as the action categories or high-level semantics.
elements.sent-2411-022: Predictions about the ongoing actions are made from partial observations of actions not yet completed.
elements.sent-2411-023: Forecasting tasks use the currently observed action(s) to reason about future actions not yet observed.
elements.sent-2411-024: We discuss relevant previous surveys for each of these three temporal scopes and overview their ocus in \Cref{tab:surveys .. Recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Early works on action recognition have primarily focused on motion modeling.
elements.sent-2411-026: As overviewed by \citet{buxton2003learning , tracking has also been applied to more complex tasks such as behavior analysis or non-verbal human interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Subsequent overviews were more task-oriented, focusing on action classification and localization \citep{weinland2011survey , behavior understanding \citep{chaaraoui2012review , and surveillance applications \citep{vishwakarma2013survey emerged based on later advancements.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Simultaneously, \citet{turaga2008machine and \citet{poppe2010survey discussed approaches addressing atomic actions and group activities.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Following surveys covered adaptations of deep learning approaches for topics such as depth-based motion recognition \citep{wang2018rgb , activity recognition \citep{beddiar2020vision , human-human interactions \citep{stergiou2019analyzing , and pose estimation \citep{zheng2020deep .}}}}

The progress of the video is indicated by the top bar. From the currently performed action of total duration $\tau_1$, only the $\tau_{1,\rho}<\tau_1 $ part is readily observable. After a transition period $0\leq\tau_{1 \rightarrow 2}$, another action is performed with duration $\tau_2$. Action recognition tasks consider full observations of the action at $\tau_1$. Action prediction uses only part $\tau_{1,\rho}$ of the ongoing action. Action forecasting uses current action at $\tau_1$ to predict future actions. elements.sent-2411-047: The diversity of the video input poses several challenges.
elements.sent-2411-048: Intra-class variations in the visual appearance of actions of the same category across videos can be due to viewpoint, occlusions, background noise, or lighting conditions.
elements.sent-2411-049: The performances and durations of actions can also significantly deviate.
elements.sent-2411-051: Training/test set instance distribution variance can also significantly impact the performance and overall generalization of the learned semantics.
elements.sent-2411-052: Challenging action instances can be traced to feature representations further from the training set distribution in such cases.
elements.sent-2411-053: Since action understanding tasks are increasingly semantic, we also face challenges in the diversity and granularity of the target outputs.
elements.sent-2411-054: Interpretation of the visual input, and sometimes the lack of observable information, increasingly requires higher-level understanding.
elements.sent-2411-055: Consequently, the relation between visual input and model output becomes more complex.
elements.sent-2411-056: Vocabulary limitations present challenges as action categories are often finite.
elements.sent-2411-057: The generalization of models to open-set or cross-domain settings primarily depends on the similarity between seen and unseen instances.
elements.sent-2411-058: Limited inter-class variation further affects good representation performance of rare coarse-grained concepts of visually similar actions.
elements.sent-2411-059: This issue is more prevalent for tasks that require fine-grained semantic granularities.

elements.sent-2411-150: The introduction of both general and task-specific datasets has improved the exploration of video tasks and standardized evaluation protocols.
elements.sent-2411-151: Main lines of research and challenges of these tasks are overviewed next.
elements.sent-2411-152: The recognition of actions in videos is a fundamental computer vision research theme.
elements.sent-2411-153: Relevant tasks focus on different aspects of the actions observed in full.
elements.sent-2411-154: We start by discussing approaches for optimizing model inputs in \Cref{sec:recognition::inputs .}
elements.sent-2411-155: We then overview popular temporal-based recognition tasks in \Cref{sec:recognition::temporal .}
elements.sent-2411-156: Tasks based on the semantic relationships between language and video are discussed in \Cref{sec:recognition::language , whereas audio-visual and other multi-modal approaches appear in \Cref{sec:recognition::audio .}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Video inputs typically consist of tens to hundreds of highly visually similar frames.
elements.sent-2411-158: The uniform use of all frames can lead to an unsustainable computational burden.
elements.sent-2411-160: Several recognition approaches in computer vision aim to reduce compute and improve memory utilization by considering inputs selectively.
elements.sent-2411-161: Reducing frame-level redundancies in videos requires a high-level understanding of each temporal segment's relevance.
elements.sent-2411-162: The distinction and selection of relevant segments directly impact information loss.
elements.sent-2411-163: Long and complex scenes present significant challenges to video reduction methods.
elements.sent-2411-164: This uneven context inclusion requires more efficient utilization of the model's capacity.

elements.sent-2411-734: To this end, we provide a comprehensive review of the broad domain of video action understanding.
elements.sent-2411-735: We discussed the main challenges, relevant datasets, and seminal works with an emphasis on recent (multi-modal) advancements across tasks, and future research directions.
elements.sent-2411-737: We focused on three temporal scopes from which tasks and approaches understand actions performed.
elements.sent-2411-738: We discussed recognition tasks that use complete observations of actions to infer fine- or coarse-grained labels.
elements.sent-2411-739: We then overviewed predictive tasks from partial observations of actions.
elements.sent-2411-740: Finally, we outlined forecasting tasks with anticipation models that infer general scene knowledge and forecast future actions not yet performed.
elements.sent-2411-741: Using time as a stepping stone, we outline current limitations and promising research directions to further advance the scope, robustness, and deployment of action understanding research.. {.}
elements.sent-2411-742: Current systems can provide coarse- and fine-grained descriptions of video scenes, extract segments corresponding to queries, synthesize unobserved parts of videos, and predict context across multiple modalities.
elements.sent-2411-743: We broadly distinguish between three temporal scopes: (1) recognition tasks of actions observed in full, (2) prediction tasks for ongoing partially observed actions, and (3) forecasting tasks for subsequent unobserved action(s).

VLM objectives align both $\mathbf{z}_v^{+}$ and $\mathbf{z}_l^{+}$. Contrastive approaches~\pcite{chen2020simple,oord2018representation,xu2021videoclip} additionally maximize the distance between negative vision-language pairs: ($\mathbf{z}_v^{+}$, $\mathbf{z}_l^{-}$) and ($\mathbf{z}_v^{-}$, $\mathbf{z}_l^{+}$).
